ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general satay entry; no pork-specific satay term exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for pork satay; closest defensible anchor is grilled pork meat products (A0CKW). Closest parent match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for pork satay as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical hawker serving of pork satay consists of 5 skewers weighing approximately 100g of cooked pork, served with peanut sauce, cucumber, and onion on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Pork Satay — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Pork Satay (cr_id F06100037). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Meat Dishes and Satay-type Preparations — Analogous Estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate basis)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for pork satay exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 (low) is estimated by analogy with marinated grilled meat dishes with moderate sugar content from peanut sauce and marinade. The carbohydrate content (15.76g/100g) is primarily from sugar in the marinade and peanut sauce; protein and fat from pork significantly blunt postprandial glucose response, supporting a low GI estimate. Glycemic load per 100g serving = (45 × 15.76) / 100 = 7.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 453mg/100g exceeds the 400mg/100g threshold; high sugar (14.52g/100g) with low fibre (0.97g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit pork satay to a small portion (3–4 skewers), request peanut sauce on the side, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium dishes in the same meal, as sodium at 453mg/100g substantially contributes to daily sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 14.52g/100g with dietary fibre only 0.97g/100g; peanut sauce and marinade contribute significant added sugars elevating postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume pork satay in moderation (3–4 skewers), request reduced sugar in the marinade and peanut sauce, avoid pairing with ketupat or other high-GI carbohydrates, and monitor postprandial glucose given the high sugar-to-fibre ratio.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~5.5g/100g, trans fat 0.1g/100g, sodium 453mg/100g, and high sugar 14.52g/100g collectively elevate cardiovascular risk; charcoal grilling generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit pork satay to occasional consumption, keep portions to 3–4 skewers, minimise peanut sauce (high in saturated fat and sodium), and avoid frequent charcoal-grilled preparations due to AGE formation and trans fat content.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork (red meat) provides arachidonic acid promoting inflammatory cytokines; high sugar (14.52g/100g), estimated saturated fat ~5.5g/100g, trans fat 0.1g/100g, and AGE generation from charcoal grilling compound pro-inflammatory burden
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit or avoid pork satay due to the combined pro-inflammatory load of red meat arachidonic acid, high sugar, saturated fat, trans fat, and charcoal-grilling AGEs, which outweigh the modest anti-inflammatory benefit of turmeric and lemongrass in the marinade.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork satay is a popular Singaporean and Malay hawker dish consisting of boneless pork pieces marinated in a spiced mixture of lemongrass, turmeric, galangal, garlic, shallots, sugar, and salt, then threaded onto bamboo skewers and grilled over charcoal. The result is tender, slightly charred pork with a smoky, sweet-savoury flavour profile and a caramelised exterior from the sugar-rich marinade. It is traditionally served with a rich peanut sauce (made from ground peanuts, coconut milk, sugar, and spices), fresh cucumber slices, and raw onion wedges, and is often accompanied by ketupat (compressed rice cakes). Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 293 kcal, 20.88g protein, 16.19g fat (estimated saturated fat ~5.5g, trans fat 0.1g), 15.76g carbohydrate, 14.52g sugar, 0.97g dietary fibre, and 453mg sodium. The high sodium content (453mg/100g) makes it a concern for hypertension management. The high sugar-to-fibre ratio (14.52g sugar vs 0.97g fibre per 100g) is relevant for Type 2 Diabetes and cardiovascular risk. The combination of red meat, high sugar, saturated fat, trans fat, and charcoal-grilling AGEs makes it a high-concern food for rheumatoid arthritis. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low) due to the protein and fat content blunting glucose absorption, with a glycemic load of approximately 7.1 per 100g serving. Pork satay is not halal and is a culturally significant dish at Singaporean hawker centres, pasar malam (night markets), and festive occasions.
common_user_queries: How many calories in pork satay?
Is pork satay suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat pork satay if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of pork satay?
How much sodium is in pork satay?
Is pork satay high in sugar?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Pork Satay (cr_id F06100037) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated at ~5.5g/100g and trans fat at 0.1g/100g based on typical pork belly/shoulder composition from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as a secondary reference for fatty acid breakdown not reported in HPB. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology; all other values are exact from HPB.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown for this specific pork satay preparation is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry, and no exact match was found in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this composite dish. 2. saturated_fat_g (5.5g/100g) and trans_fat_g (0.1g/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for grilled pork shoulder/belly as the closest approved-source analogue; these values are consistent with the input health_suitability notes. 3. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct measurement for pork satay exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. 4. Pork satay is not halal (pork product); is_halal is set to false. 5. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are approximate matches; no exact ontology codes for pork satay exist in FoodOn or FoodEx2 as of 2026-04-16.
